blocks.hero_h1: Import a 2018 Tesla Model S P100D to NZ
blocks.hero_subtitle: Bring a 2018 Tesla Model S P100D into NZ with clear landed costs, a focus on battery health, and support from an NZ-based team that understands premium EVs.
proof_points: Transparent pricing — compliance is part of the process
blocks.why_kiwis_buy: Kiwis look at the 2018 Tesla Model S P100D for long-distance EV commuting, quiet motorway trips and low day-to-day running costs. It suits drivers who want strong motorway performance, space for family or clients, and the ability to charge at home instead of stopping at stations.

savings_block.headline: What a 2018 Model S P100D can cost landed in NZ
savings_block.body: Recent 2018 Tesla Model S P100D examples from Japan have typically landed in NZ between about $30,000 and $75,000, depending on mileage, condition, options and auction price. Local dealer stock often sits higher once margins are added. We map out the full landed cost — auction price, shipping, insurance, NZ entry certification, on-road costs and our fee — so you can compare importing against buying off a yard.

cta_form.headline: Find your Model S P100D — we'll do the searching
cta_form.subtext: Fill in your details to get started. We'll search the Japanese auctions and come back with real options and pricing by email.

blocks.video_caption: Take a closer look at the 2018 Tesla Model S P100D — note the low, wide stance and large touchscreen that defines the cabin.
blocks.main_usp_deep: The 2018 Tesla Model S P100D pairs a large battery with a dual-motor powertrain and fast DC charging, which works well for NZ-wide driving. You get instant response for overtaking, plus the ability to cover long distances with planned charging stops. Most owners charge overnight at home, then top up on public fast chargers on longer trips. For many NZ drivers, that means lower running costs and fewer stops on the road.

buyer_persona.headline: Is this car right for you?
buyer_persona.body: You might be a professional doing regular trips between cities, a family wanting a safe, quick EV with real boot space, or an EV fan stepping up from a smaller hatch. If you value strong performance, quiet travel and the ability to charge at home, a 2018 Tesla Model S P100D is a solid match.

trust_company.headline: Why import with ImportYourCar.co.nz?
trust_company.body: We're an NZ-based team that has been helping buyers bring cars in from Japan since 2009. We focus on clear communication, full auction documentation and realistic landed cost estimates before you commit. For EVs like the Tesla Model S P100D, we pay particular attention to battery health information and usage history. You stay in control of the decisions while we handle the legwork in Japan and on the NZ side.

car_specs_benefits.benefit: Gives you access to a well-known premium EV with strong performance and long-distance capability for NZ roads.

car_specs_benefits.benefit: Helps you understand real-world range and likely degradation before the car leaves Japan.
Delivers confident traction in wet conditions and strong acceleration for motorway on-ramps and passing lanes.
Lets you top up quickly on NZ's growing fast-charger network during longer trips.

car_specs_benefits.spec: Large liftback body with generous boot
car_specs_benefits.benefit: Makes it easier to carry luggage, prams or work gear without moving to an SUV.

cta_mid_page.headline: Start a search for your 2018 Model S P100D

models_comparison.intro: Here’s how the 2018 Tesla Model S P100D compares across key points when importing from Japan.

rows.notes: The 2018 Model S P100D sits within the 2018–2025 window for this shape, so it typically meets NZ entry standards on age and safety. Later years may add software and feature updates but usually cost more at auction.

Within the Model S range there are different battery sizes and motor outputs. The P100D is one of the higher-capacity options, offering strong acceleration and longer range, which suits NZ intercity driving if you plan your charging.

Spec can vary by market and original buyer options — things like interior materials, audio, driver assistance features and wheel size. Higher-spec cars tend to command higher auction prices but may not change day-to-day usability much for some buyers.

As a used EV, the 2018 Model S P100D is subject to current NZ safety and entry rules, and to any applicable CO2 or road-user charges policy at the time it lands. Based on auction documentation, this model usually meets NZ entry standards if documentation is clean and no modifications are noted.

The Model S P100D appears regularly in major Japanese auctions, but clean, low-kilometre examples are fewer and can attract stronger bidding. That can mean waiting a little longer to find the right car at the right price.

steps.title: Tell us what you're after
steps.body: Fill in the form or message us. We confirm your brief — budget, mileage, colour preferences, features — and give you a cost breakdown before anything moves forward.

steps.title: We search the auctions
steps.body: We monitor USS, TAA, HAA and others. For a Tesla Model S P100D, we pay close attention to battery health notes, usage history and any mention of modifications on the auction sheet before shortlisting.

steps.title: You approve, we bid
steps.body: We send you the auction sheet, photos and our comments on condition and value. Once you're happy, you pay a deposit and we bid on your behalf.

steps.title: Shipping to NZ
steps.body: Typically 2–4 weeks depending on the sailing schedule. For less common specs or colours, allow a little extra time once the auction clears. We keep you updated along the way.

steps.title: Compliance and delivery
steps.body: The car goes through NZ entry certification and compliance. Once it's through, we arrange delivery or you can collect.

risks_solutions.intro: Importing a 2018 Tesla Model S P100D is straightforward, but it helps to know the common questions and risks.

items.risk: Worried about buying an EV from overseas without seeing the battery health in person?
items.solution: We source full auction documentation and, for this model, include a battery health report with every vehicle. You see the data and our comments before you decide whether to bid.

items.risk: Concerned about hidden costs between the auction price and the keys in your hand?
items.solution: We lay out a landed cost estimate that covers auction price, shipping, insurance, NZ entry certification, on-road costs and our fee, so you can see where your money goes.

items.risk: Unsure how to judge auction grades and condition on a high-value EV?
items.solution: We explain the auction grade, translate key notes and highlight any red flags such as repairs or modifications. You get the same sheets we see, plus our view on whether the car in question typically meets NZ entry standards if documentation is clean and no modifications are noted.

blocks.how_to_use_iyc: You tell us the year, budget and features you want in a 2018 Tesla Model S P100D, and we watch the Japanese auctions for matching cars. We check documentation, battery health information and pricing, then send you options with full landed cost estimates. If one looks right, we handle the bidding, shipping and NZ side while you stay in the loop.

faq_block.q: What sort of range can I expect from a 2018 Tesla Model S P100D in NZ?
faq_block.a: Real-world range depends on driving style, terrain and battery health, but many owners see less than the original brochure figures. With a healthy battery and mixed NZ driving, you can plan for comfortable intercity trips with a charging stop along the way.

faq_block.q: How much does it cost to import a 2018 Tesla Model S P100D from Japan?
faq_block.a: Most buyers land these between about $30,000 and $75,000, depending on mileage, condition and auction price. On top of the hammer price, you need to allow for shipping, insurance, NZ entry certification, on-road costs and our service fee.

faq_block.q: Will a 2018 Tesla Model S P100D meet NZ entry standards?
faq_block.a: Based on auction documentation, this model usually meets NZ entry standards if documentation is clean and no modifications are noted. Final entry certification is done in NZ, so we can't confirm the outcome until the car has been inspected here.

faq_block.q: How long does it take from winning an auction to driving the car in NZ?
faq_block.a: From a successful bid, shipping to NZ typically takes 2–4 weeks depending on sailings, then you need time for clearance, entry certification and registration. In most cases, buyers are driving their cars within about 6–10 weeks of auction day.

faq_block.q: How do auction grades relate to the condition of a used Tesla?
faq_block.a: Auction grades are a shorthand for overall condition, but the notes and photos matter just as much. We translate the sheet, point out repairs, wear and any mention of battery or charging issues, then give you our view so you can decide whether the car suits your expectations.

blocks.company_positioning: ImportYourCar.co.nz is an NZ-based import specialist working directly with Japanese auctions since 2009. We help buyers source EVs, hybrids and other vehicles with clear documentation and realistic landed cost estimates. Our team focuses on matching the right car to each brief rather than pushing in-house stock.

landed_cost_breakdown.intro: Your landed cost is the total to get a 2018 Tesla Model S P100D from a Japanese auction to registered on NZ roads.

rows.notes: The hammer price you pay at the Japanese auction, influenced by mileage, condition, options and demand on the day.
Transport from the Japanese port to NZ and cover for the car while it’s on the water.
NZ entry certification, inspections, any required work to meet standards, registration and plates, plus our service fee.

auction_checklist.why: Battery condition has a big impact on real-world range and long-term running costs for a 2018 Model S P100D.
Frequent fast charging or heavy high-speed use can affect long-term battery health.
Gives you a sense of how the car has been treated and whether any structural repairs are noted.
Important for NZ entry standards and long-term durability, especially around suspension and battery mounting points.
High use can show up in seat wear and touchscreen condition, which affects day-to-day enjoyment.

not_for_you_block.headline: Who this may not suit
items: You want a low upfront price and aren't comfortable with the extra steps involved in importing from Japan.
You rarely drive beyond short city trips and might be better served by a smaller, cheaper EV.
You prefer a higher seating position and easier access, in which case a premium EV SUV could be a better fit.

alternatives_block.intro: If a 2018 Tesla Model S P100D isn’t quite right, there are other premium EVs worth considering.

rows.better_if: You want a newer-feeling cabin and slightly smaller car that’s easier to park in tight city spots.
rows.tradeoff: Less boot space and rear headroom compared with the Model S liftback body.

rows.better_if: You mainly drive in and around town and want a lower-cost EV with reasonable range.
rows.tradeoff: Not as quick or spacious as a Model S, and feels less premium inside.

rows.better_if: You prefer a premium SUV layout with higher seating and a European badge.
rows.tradeoff: Typically higher landed cost and different charging and range profile compared with the P100D.

battery_health_block.headline: Battery health on imported 2018 Model S P100D examples
battery_health_block.body: For a 2018 Tesla Model S P100D, battery health is central to value and day-to-day usability. Japanese auction sheets and service records can give useful clues about degradation, charging habits and any past issues. We focus on cars with clear documentation and include a battery health report with every vehicle, so you can weigh up range and long-term ownership before you commit.
checklist: Review battery health report and any capacity estimates provided
Check for notes on charging behaviour, such as frequent fast charging
Look for warning history or battery-related repairs in service records
Consider mileage in context with battery health, not on its own
Balance price against expected remaining usable capacity for your driving

charging_block.headline: Charging a 2018 Tesla Model S P100D in NZ
charging_block.home_charging: Most NZ owners charge a Model S P100D at home overnight using a dedicated wall unit or suitable socket, topping up the battery while the car sits idle. That routine usually covers daily commuting and school runs without needing public chargers.
charging_block.public_charging: For longer trips, you can use NZ’s growing network of public AC and DC fast chargers. The P100D’s fast-charging capability means you can add useful range during a coffee or lunch stop, as long as you plan your route around available chargers.
caveats: Check what charging hardware and cables are supplied with the car from Japan.
Allow for the cost of installing a home wall charger if you don’t already have one.
Rural routes may have fewer fast chargers, so plan ahead for long-distance travel.
Charging speeds can vary depending on battery temperature and state of charge.
